name: Stirling Old Town Walk
title: The walk, stop by stop
note: Photos of each stop on the optional walk down from the castle through Stirling's old town to the Old Bridge. Tap any picture for the story and its source.

items.caption: Broad Street, the wide market spine of the old town. Everything on this walk lies along the ridge running down from the castle to the river.
items.kidcaption: Broad Street, the old town's main street. The whole walk runs downhill from the castle to the river along this ridge.

items.caption: Inside the Church of the Holy Rude. In 1567 the infant James VI, Mary's son, was crowned King of Scots here, with John Knox preaching the sermon.
items.kidcaption: Inside the town church. A baby king, James, was crowned right here, just down the hill from where his mum Mary had been crowned in the castle.
items.credit: Robert Cutts from Bristol, England, UK

items.caption: The Church of the Holy Rude from outside, one of very few churches in Britain to have hosted a coronation, with a medieval graveyard spilling down the slope.
items.kidcaption: The town church from outside. Look for the old, old gravestones tumbling down the hill beside it.

items.caption: The Old Town Jail, a stern Victorian prison of 1847, now toured with costumed guides. Before it, prisoners were held in the grim old Tolbooth down the street.
items.kidcaption: The Old Town Jail. Costumed guides will show you the cold cells and act out just how tough life was for the prisoners here.

items.caption: Mar's Wark, the carved facade of a grand town house begun around 1570 by the Earl of Mar, hereditary keeper of the castle. Never finished, now a striking shell.
items.kidcaption: Mar's Wark. A powerful earl started building this fancy house about 450 years ago, but it was never finished, so now it is a spooky stone shell.

items.caption: Argyll's Lodging, the finest surviving 17th-century town house of its kind in Scotland: how the nobility lived in town, in comfort, just below the fortress.
items.kidcaption: Argyll's Lodging, a rich noble's town house. This is how the fanciest families lived when they came down from the castle into town.

items.caption: The unicorn atop the Mercat Cross, nicknamed 'the Puggy'. The market cross was the heart of town business, proclamations, and public punishments.
items.kidcaption: The unicorn on top of the market cross has a nickname, 'the Puggy'. This spot was where markets, news, and even punishments happened.

items.caption: The Tolbooth, the old town hall, courthouse and jail rolled into one, where the burgh governed itself, collected its dues, and locked up wrongdoers.
items.kidcaption: The Tolbooth was the town's town hall, court, and jail all in one building. If you broke the rules, this is where you ended up.

items.caption: The end of the walk: the Old Stirling Bridge, the 15th-century stone successor to the timber bridge where Wallace sprang his trap in 1297.
items.kidcaption: The walk ends at the old stone bridge. The wooden bridge from Wallace's famous trap in 1297 stood right next to it.
